content.full_text:
May 19, 1975


George H. Murphy
Legislative Counsel
of California
3021 State Capitol
Sacramento, Calif. 95814

Re: Request for Opinion
No. 75-113

Dear Mr. Murphy:

Thank you for your letter of May 5, 1975,
requesting an opinion under the Political Reform Act.
You asked whether the Political Reform Act prohibits
a member of the legislature from accepting a plaque
awarded to him or her by a city chamber of commerce,
and if not, does the Act require the legislator to
disclose his acceptance of the plaque. The estimated
value of the plaque is fifty dollars.

Since your request raises no substantial
question involving interpretation of the Political
Reform Act, your request for a formal opinion is
denied. The Political Reform Act does not prohibit
a legislator from accepting a plaque from a city
chamber of commerce. The only situation in which
such an award would be prohibited is in the unlikely
case where a city chamber of commerce is an employer
of a lobbyist, and the lobbyist arranges for the
making of the award. In such a case, Government
Code Section 86203 prohibits the making of a gift
valued at more than $10.00, and Section 86204 makes
it unlawful for a person to knowingly receive such
a gift.

Although the legislator can accept the plaque,
receipt of the plaque must be reported on the legislator's statement of economic interests (Form 720),
because the fair market value of the plaque is more
than $25.00. Section 87207 requires that the legislator disclose the name, address, and general description of the business activity of the source of any

George H. Murphy
May 19, 1975
Page 2


gift worth more than twenty-five dollars. The state-
ment must also include the value of the gift and the
date on which it was received.

Although no formal opinion will be issued, I hope
the foregoing answers your questions and will be suf-
ficiently helpful. If you have any further questions,
I urge you to contact a member of the Commission staff.

Sincerely,


Daniel A. Lowenstein
Chairman

DAL:jp

BERNARD CZEOLA
CHIEF DEPUTY

OWEN C. KUNG
EDWIN C. PRIESELL
RAYMOND C. RICE

L. DECHAMBEAU
JAY M. KUNZI
JAY M. MEREDITH
WILLIAM MCKINZIE, JR.
ANN M. MACKEY
EDWARD R. NOMURA
RUSSELL L. SPARLING
PRINCIPAL DEPUTIES

2021 STATE CAPITOL
SACRAMENTO 95814

107 SOUTH BROADWAY
LOS ANGELES 90012

Legislative Counsel
of California

GEORGE H. MURPHY

Sacramento, California
May 5, 1975

Fair Political Practices Commission
926 J Street, Room 522
Sacramento, California 95814

Request No. 75-1

Dear Commissioners:

Pursuant to Section 83114 of the Government Code, I request your opinion on the following questions:

1. Does any provision of the Political Reform Act of 1974 prohibit a member of the Legislature from accepting a plaque awarded to him by a city chamber of commerce?

The estimated value of the plaque is $50.

2. If the answer to the first question is no, does the act require the legislator to disclose his acceptance of the plaque? If so, what specific information must be disclosed?

I am requesting this opinion in my capacity as attorney for a member of the Legislature.

Very truly yours,

George H. Murphy
Legislative Counsel

Murphy Legislative Counsel of California 3021 State Capitol Sacramento, Calif. 95814 Re: Request for Opinion No. 75-113 Dear Mr. Murphy: Thank you for your letter of May 5, 1975, requesting an opinion under the Political Reform Act. You asked whether the Political Reform Act prohibits a member of the legislature from accepting a plaque awarded to him or her by a city chamber of commerce, and if not, does the Act require the legislator to disclose his acceptance of the plaque. The estimated value of the plaque is fifty dollars. Since your request raises no substantial question involving interpretation of the Political Reform Act, your request for a formal opinion is denied. The Political Reform Act does not prohibit a legislator from accepting a plaque from a city chamber of commerce. The only situation in which such an award would be prohibited is in the unlikely case where a city chamber of commerce is an employer of a lobbyist, and the lobbyist arranges for the making of the award. In such a case, Government Code Section 86203 prohibits the making of a gift valued at more than $10.00, and Section 86204 makes it unlawful for a person to knowingly receive such a gift. Although the legislator can accept the plaque, receipt of the plaque must be reported on the legislator's statement of economic interests (Form 720), because the fair market value of the plaque is more than $25.00. Section 87207 requires that the legislator disclose the name, address, and general description of the business activity of the source of any gift worth more than twenty-five dollars. The statement must also include the value of the gift and the date on which it was received. Although no formal opinion will be issued, I hope the foregoing answers your questions and will be sufficiently helpful.
